evidence: Constructor override handling is inconsistent: `wifi_runtime_api.go` uses `NewWiFiRuntimeService` that applies overrides directly (`runtimeInstance = NewWiFiRuntimeWith(runtime[0])`), while `prepareForUse` only repairs `RunCommand` and assumes other function fields remain valid.

`wifi_runtime_methods.go` hard-codes low-level command strings (`nmcli`, `ip`, `sysctl`) and field-index parsing (`parts[7]`) in the runtime layer, so external command volatility leaks directly into service behavior.

`resolveDevice` and runtime state helpers resolve device names through `state` + runtime command output, but there is no formal seam between command-format concerns and domain logic in the service API.
README.md declares contract compatibility and lifecycle assertions for protocol/StarTram surfaces and ties them to a runtime contract gate
The CI gate script invoked by that workflow (upload-contract-gate.yml) runs only a narrow named test subset in `goseg/protocol/contracts`, not all contract-definition tests present in that package

goseg/broadcast/state.go carries `broadcastStateRuntime` fields that are initialized but never used for reads/writes (`urbitTransitions`, `urbTransMu`, `sysTransMu`), indicating an over-retained abstraction shape that is no longer coupled to runtime behavior.

`AddSystemTransitionError` in goseg/broadcast/state.go prepends every broadcast transition error with `append` and never bounds the retained history, so repeated transition failures can grow state unbounded in the long-lived broadcast state.

protocol/contracts/contracts.go does an O(1) map lookup for a binding ID and then linearly scans the binding slice to validate/return the same binding, coupling two parallel lookup representations without a reason to keep both hot paths.
design_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: Design debt is concentrated in state/catalog lookup layering: partially retired fields and duplicate lookup structures increase maintenance risk and make transition/error behavior harder to reason about. Streamline these abstractions before raising coherence score.

findings.summary: Runtime override API allows partially initialized runtime objects to bypass defaulting, creating an unsafe abstraction boundary for dependency injection.
evidence: `NewWiFiRuntimeService(runtime ...wifiRuntime)` replaces the default runtime with `runtime[0]` directly, without merging with defaults.
`prepareForUse()` only repopulates `RunCommand` when nil and does not validate `NewWifiClient`, `ClientInterfacesFn`, `ClientBSSFn`, or `RunNmcliFn`.
`disconnect()` unconditionally calls `runtime.NewWifiClient()` through `client, err := runtime.NewWifiClient()`; a partially populated override can crash with nil-function panic paths.
findings.suggestion: Make the service use one canonical resolver path for runtime defaults (e.g., always call `resolveWiFiRuntime` in constructor and tests), and validate all injectable seams in `prepareForUse` with fallback defaults or explicit errors for each critical function field before use. This should be a module-level contract change so any override must either be complete or intentionally partial via a dedicated test builder.

findings.summary: Wi-Fi runtime abstraction leaks nmcli output structure directly into logic, weakening interface honesty and long-term evolution.
evidence: `parseNmcliWifiSSIDs` extracts SSIDs by splitting raw nmcli output on `:` and reading fixed index `parts[7]`.
`parseNmcliWifiDevices` reads fixed positional fields (`parts[0]`, `parts[1]`) from `nmcli -t -f DEVICE,TYPE` output with no version-aware schema contract.
`connect`, `listSSIDs`, and `toggleDevice` invoke shell commands as strings (`nmcli`, `ip`) through the runtime, but results are not normalized via domain types.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a typed command-output parser/adapter contract that returns explicit domain models (`WifiDevice`, `WifiScanResult`) and explicit parser implementations behind the runtime boundary; include version/format guards and return structured parse errors instead of slicing positional columns inline.

findings.summary: Contract gate does not execute per-contract definition regression tests, reducing deterministic contract-coverage confidence
evidence: The README positions contract surfaces as governance-critical and requires lifecycle-policy assertions for changed catalog entries
The contract gate path list includes `protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go`, but the script it runs filters tests with a narrow `go test ./protocol/contracts -run` pattern
findings.suggestion: Update the runtime contract gate test command set to include contract-definition regression tests for protocol and StarTram contract files (for example exact descriptor/name/message/metadata snapshots), or replace the regex with a package-level test selection that always runs deterministic per-family contract-definition tests when those contract files change.

findings.summary: Remove dead transition state from broadcast runtime before it becomes a permanent abstraction boundary
evidence: `broadcastStateRuntime` defines `urbitTransitions`, `urbTransMu`, and `sysTransMu` fields, and constructors initialize transition maps/mutexes, but no methods in `state.go` read or write through those fields.
The runtime methods in `state.go`/`loop.go` interact with state broadcast collection, scheduled packs, and collectors only; transition maps and their locks are functionally inert.
findings.suggestion: Refactor `broadcastStateRuntime` to remove unused transition map/lock fields (or reintroduce a single explicit transition-transition API if that behavior is still required). This reduces hidden state surface and clarifies runtime ownership.

findings.summary: Bound transition error history to prevent broadcast state from growing indefinitely
evidence: `AddSystemTransitionError` prepends each new message into `runtime.broadcastState.System.Transition.Error` using `append` without any cap.
`runBroadcastTickWithRuntime` writes into this same error channel on each tick failure, so sustained failures can continuously extend in-memory state.
findings.suggestion: Store transition errors in a bounded ring/capped slice (for example last N entries). Preserve newest/most important messages and drop oldest entries on each append to avoid unbounded memory growth in long-lived processes.

findings.summary: Eliminate redundant linear scan in action-contract binding lookup
evidence: `ActionContractBindingFor` performs a map lookup to get `contractID`, then loops over `actionContractBindings` to find the matching binding.
Tests already assert deterministic contract behavior in multiple paths, so this behavior is exercised and can be simplified without changing observed semantics.
findings.suggestion: Store and return binding objects directly from the existing binding map (or add a second binding map) and remove the slice walk. Keep sorted slice construction as a separate read-only projection for deterministic iteration, not the primary lookup path.
